planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or plant nursery transplants in spring; broad-leaved thyme is the most aromatic of the European thymes, with larger leaves than common thyme that hold their flavor better when dried, and a sprawling habit that makes it excellent for paths and edges.

directions: Start seeds indoors or direct sow after last frost in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thyme is intolerant of wet, heavy soils and will rot quickly in poorly drained ground. Thymus pulegioides is the broad-leaved thyme of European meadows and hedgerows, producing larger, broader leaves than common thyme (T. vulgaris) with a pungent, highly aromatic thymol fragrance that is considered by many herb growers to be the most intensely flavored thyme species for cooking; the broader leaves also dry and crumble more easily than narrow-leaved types. The sprawling, somewhat woody stems root where they touch the soil, making it an effective aromatic path-side groundcover, and the pink to lilac flowers in summer are heavily visited by bees. Harvest before full bloom for the most concentrated flavor; cut stems back by half after flowering to keep plants compact.

planting.note: Plant root divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; eleuthero is a hardy adaptogenic medicinal shrub of eastern Asian forests used for centuries in Chinese and Russian herbal medicine, with well-documented studies on its stress-adaptogenic properties.

directions: Plant root divisions or hardwood cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; eleuthero is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and tolerates drought once established. Eleutherococcus senticosus (commonly marketed as Siberian Ginseng, though not a true Panax ginseng) is a thorny, deciduous shrub of northeastern Asia that is one of the most studied adaptogenic medicinal plants; its root bark is used in clinical herbalism as a tonic to improve stress resilience, physical endurance, and immune function, with hundreds of published studies supporting its use in Russian and Chinese traditional medicine. The dried root bark is made into tea or tincture; harvest bark from roots of 3–5 year old plants in autumn. The plant is fully hardy, grows readily in cool northern climates, and is far easier to cultivate than true ginseng. Note: consult a qualified herbalist before medicinal use.
sources: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center Herb Information

planting.note: Sow stratified seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; ginseng requires 2 years of stratification for reliable germination and is typically grown from pre-stratified seed or 2-year rootlets; roots are not harvested until the plant is 5–7 years old.

directions: Plant pre-stratified seeds or 2-year rootlets in fall in moist, deeply humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil under 70–80% shade from deciduous trees; ginseng is extremely slow and demanding, needing years of careful growing before harvest. Asian ginseng is one of the most prized medicinal herbs in East Asian traditional medicine, used for thousands of years as a general tonic and adaptogen; commercially grown in Korea and China where demand far exceeds supply from wild populations. Home cultivation requires patience — seeds need 18 months of stratification, plants take 5–7 years to produce marketable roots, and they are highly susceptible to damping-off, voles, and deer. Excellent drainage is critical; plant in raised woodland beds with 50% shade cloth or under deciduous trees on a north-facing slope. Consult a qualified herbalist before medicinal use of the root.
sources: Penn State Extension Ginseng Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Amaranthus dubius is one of the primary leafy vegetables of tropical and subtropical Africa, Asia, and the Caribbean and is exceptionally heat-tolerant — it thrives in Gulf Coast summer conditions when most cool-season greens have bolted and failed.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on the soil surface and press in gently — amaranth seeds need light to germinate and should not be buried more than 1/8 inch deep; thin to 6–12 inches when plants are 3 inches tall. Amaranthus dubius is the primary edible-leaf amaranth of the Caribbean and tropical Africa, where it is known as callaloo, bhaji, or Chinese spinach and harvested as a highly nutritious leafy green with more than twice the iron, calcium, and protein of spinach per serving; it is one of the few leafy vegetables that produces abundantly through Gulf Coast and tropical zone summers when temperatures exceed 95°F and humidity is high. Harvest young leaves and shoot tips from the time plants are 6 inches tall; regular cutting encourages bushy growth and delays flowering. The entire young plant can be cooked like spinach in soups, stews, and sautés.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Vegetables Extension

planting.note: Self-sows freely throughout gardens — typically not intentionally cultivated but harvested as a wild edible; yellow wood sorrel naturalizes in disturbed soil, lawn edges, and garden beds across the eastern US and makes a refreshing wild salad herb when leaves are young and tender.

directions: Yellow wood sorrel is a native perennial that self-sows throughout gardens and lawns without cultivation; it is included here as a wild edible and forager's herb rather than a planted crop. Oxalis stricta produces the classic shamrock-shaped, clover-like trifoliate leaves and tiny yellow five-petaled flowers that are familiar to any gardener as a common weed; the young leaves and stems have a pleasant, sour, lemon-like flavor from oxalic acid that is refreshing in small quantities in salads, garnishes, and herb butters. Children particularly enjoy the tangy flavor of the raw leaves. Foragers note: consume in moderation as large quantities of oxalic acid can irritate the kidneys; avoid if prone to kidney stones. The small seed pods explode when ripe, launching seeds several feet — the primary reason it spreads so freely.
sources: Foraging Texas: Samuel Thayer's Field Guide
